stories.headline: Grass fire near Thornton shuts down stretch of I-25, prompts evacuations before being contained
stories.summary:
A grass fire that ignited around noon Tuesday in the Sherrelwood area of Thornton forced the closure of Interstate 25 between Thornton Parkway and 84th Avenue as thick smoke billowed across the roadway, severely limiting visibility. The fire burned near Pinnacle High School at 84th and Huron Street, prompting the evacuation of the school along with nearby businesses and homes. Police conducted door-to-door evacuations in neighborhoods to the northeast of the fire, directing displaced residents to Water World as a safe gathering point.

By 2:05 p.m., Thornton Fire Department officials confirmed the fire had been contained and crews had transitioned to cooling hot spots. Interstate 25 fully reopened following the containment. Thornton Fire Chief Steve Kelley said the fire burned approximately 10 acres in the northern Denver metro area.

The fire broke out amid a Red Flag Warning in effect across the Front Range due to dangerous combinations of high winds, low humidity, and dry vegetation. The Thornton Police and Fire departments continued to post updates on social media as evacuated residents awaited clearance to return to their homes.

stories.headline: Denver drops Flock Safety contract, proposes new license plate camera deal with Axon
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston announced Tuesday that the city will end its controversial relationship with Flock Safety and instead pursue a new license plate-reading camera contract with Axon, which already supplies the Denver Police Department with body camera technology. The announcement follows months of public pressure from hundreds of residents who objected to Flock's reported cooperation with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement in support of the Trump administration's deportation operations.

The proposed Axon contract includes several safeguards intended to address civil liberties concerns. Image retention would be reduced from 30 days under Flock to 21 days, and Axon has agreed not to share data with ICE. Unlike Flock, Axon does not maintain a national database accessible to other law enforcement or federal agencies. Cameras would capture only vehicles and license plates, not faces.

City Council members, some of whom publicly criticized the Flock arrangement, will review the proposed contract in coming weeks. Councilwoman Flor Alvidrez said she was cautiously optimistic but remained concerned about the collection of data on vehicles not suspected of any crime. Johnston acknowledged that some residents oppose any camera system, but said his responsibility is to balance civil liberties protection with public safety.

stories.headline: Greeley voters reject $1.1 billion Cascadia development project in special election
stories.summary:
Greeley voters on Tuesday approved a ballot measure repealing the city ordinance that had authorized the $1.1 billion Cascadia development project, halting what would have been one of the most ambitious mixed-use projects in northern Colorado history. Unofficial results showed 11,342 votes in favor of repeal versus 9,506 against, a margin of roughly 54 to 46 percent. Official results are expected to be posted March 5.

The Cascadia project, proposed by Water Valley CEO Martin Lind, would have included an 8,600-seat multiuse arena intended to house the Colorado Eagles — a minor-league affiliate of the Colorado Avalanche — along with a full-service conference hotel, an indoor waterpark, and housing developments spanning more than 800 acres in West Greeley. The city had planned to finance the project through bonding, general improvement district financing, and operating revenues.

Opponents organized under the banner Greeley Demands Better gathered more than 4,500 signatures to trigger the special election, citing concerns about financial risk to taxpayers. Co-chair Brandon Wark called the outcome a historic victory for residents who felt shut out by a 5-2 city council vote that originally approved the project. Developer Martin Lind and his companies filed a lawsuit Monday seeking to void the special election; that litigation remains pending following Tuesday's results. With the repeal, the land reverts to agricultural zoning.

stories.headline: Speed cameras to go live on I-25 between Mead and Berthoud starting next week
stories.summary:
Colorado will deploy eight automated speed enforcement cameras along a stretch of Interstate 25 between Mead and Berthoud beginning March 1, marking the first use of such cameras on a Colorado interstate. The cameras are positioned within an active construction zone where workers are building new express lanes in each direction. Drivers caught exceeding the 65 mph speed limit will receive warning notices for approximately one month before civil fines of $75 take effect, likely around April 2.

CDOT officials said camera tests conducted over the past month found that roughly 10 percent of drivers exceeded the posted limit by more than 10 mph. The agency cited data from a comparable deployment along Colorado 119 between Boulder and Longmont, where 34,000 warnings were issued between July and December 2025 and officials observed an 80 percent decrease in high-speed incidents after the warning period began.

Speed was a contributing factor in more than 1,200 crashes and 91 fatalities on Colorado roads in 2025, according to state records. Revenue from fines will primarily fund the speed enforcement program, with excess proceeds directed toward protecting vulnerable road users. CDOT officials said expansion of the speed camera program to other interstate corridors is under consideration, though no timetable has been set.

stories.headline: Denver AI firm Rank One Computing goes public on Nasdaq, raises $24 million
stories.summary:
Rank One Computing, a Denver-based artificial intelligence company specializing in image recognition, began trading on the Nasdaq Capital Market under the ticker ROC on Friday, raising $24 million in its initial public offering at $6 per share. Demand was strong enough to trigger an overallotment option allowing the underwriter to purchase an additional 600,000 shares within 30 days. Shares closed Tuesday at $5.80, giving the company a market value of approximately $95 million.

Founded in 2015, ROC has 42 employees and focuses on a narrow but technically demanding niche: recognizing faces, fingerprints, irises, tattoos, license plates, and weapons. The company's Vision AI platform is designed to allow machines to interpret visual information without requiring expensive graphics processing units, making the technology more deployable in field settings and within constrained government budgets. Customers include U.S. federal agencies, state and local public safety organizations, global financial institutions, and commercial security firms.

The IPO comes just days after Palantir Technologies, a far larger AI company, announced it was relocating from Denver to Miami. ROC is the second Colorado company to go public in 2026, following satellite maker York Space Systems of Greenwood Village, which raised $629 million in January. ROC said it plans to use proceeds to expand engineering, sales, and support teams, and to accelerate training and deployment of its AI algorithms.

stories.headline: High winds ground flights at DIA, causing hundreds of delays and cancellations
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport experienced a temporary ground stop Tuesday afternoon as sustained winds of up to 37 mph and gusts reaching 47 mph disrupted flight operations across the Denver metro area. Federal Aviation Administration officials lifted the ground stop but warned additional stoppages or delays remained possible into the evening. As of 5:35 p.m., FlightAware data showed 386 flights delayed and 25 canceled.

SkyWest Airlines reported 119 delays, United Airlines logged 100 delays and 12 cancellations, and Southwest Airlines recorded 108 delays and six cancellations. The National Weather Service issued a Red Flag Warning for the Front Range and Eastern Plains due to the combination of high winds, low relative humidity, and dry conditions — the same conditions that fueled a grass fire in Thornton earlier in the day.

The wind event was part of a broader period of extreme weather that forecasters had warned could bring gusts up to 80 mph in some areas of Colorado. Travelers were advised to check with their airlines for the latest flight status information.

stories.headline: Denver police searching for three armed robbery suspects believed to be driving Ford Explorer
stories.summary:
Denver police are asking for the public's help identifying three individuals suspected of committing armed robberies in the city. Investigators believe the trio may have been traveling in a Ford Explorer that potentially had a handicapped placard hanging from the rearview mirror.

No additional details about the specific locations or dates of the alleged robberies were immediately available from the department. Police have not announced any arrests in connection with the incidents.

Anyone with information about the suspects or the vehicle is asked to contact the Denver Police Department or Metro Denver Crime Stoppers, which accepts anonymous tips.

stories.headline: Suspect in Denver Valentine's Day weekend party shooting arrested in Kansas
stories.summary:
A man suspected of fatally shooting a person at a Denver party during Valentine's Day weekend was arrested in Kansas, approximately 600 miles from the scene of the crime, according to Denver police. The arrest was confirmed Tuesday as investigators continue to piece together the circumstances of the shooting.

Denver police had been searching for the suspect since the killing occurred at a party in Denver more than a week ago. Details on the specific charges filed and whether extradition proceedings are underway were not immediately released.

The case underscores ongoing concerns about gun violence at social gatherings in the Denver metro area. Additional suspects, if any, have not been publicly identified. Authorities have not released the victim's name.

stories.headline: Nuggets fall to shorthanded Golden State Warriors despite Jokic triple-double
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets suffered an upset loss to the Golden State Warriors Tuesday despite a dominant triple-double performance from star center Nikola Jokic. The Warriors were playing shorthanded for the game, making the defeat a significant concern for a Nuggets team already under scrutiny for inconsistent play.

The loss adds to Denver's recent pattern of struggles against teams they would be expected to handle comfortably, compounding concerns about the team's readiness for the postseason. Jokic's individual excellence has not been enough to mask larger team-wide deficiencies, particularly on the defensive end.

The Nuggets continue to navigate a difficult stretch of the regular season with playoff seeding implications on the line. Denver's coaching staff and front office face mounting pressure to address the team's tendency to drop games that should be within reach.
